sections.heading: 1. Purpose
sections.content:
This policy establishes the organisation's commitment to systematic risk management in accordance with ISO 31000:2018 Risk Management — Guidelines. It defines the principles, framework, and process for managing risk across all activities, functions, and projects.

The policy ensures that risk management is integrated into governance, strategy, planning, management, reporting, policies, values, and culture.

sections.heading: 2. Scope
sections.content: This policy applies to all employees, contractors, and third parties operating within the organisation. It covers all types of risk including but not limited to:

sections.heading: 3. Policy Statement
sections.content:
The organisation is committed to managing risk proactively and systematically to protect and create value for stakeholders. Risk management shall be:

a) Integrated — embedded in all organisational activities
b) Structured and comprehensive — contributing to consistent and comparable results
c) Customised — proportionate and aligned to context
d) Inclusive — involving appropriate and timely stakeholder engagement
e) Dynamic — anticipating, detecting, acknowledging, and responding to changes
f) Based on the best available information
g) Considerate of human and cultural factors
h) Subject to continual improvement

sections.heading: 4. Risk Management Principles (ISO 31000 Cl. 4)
sections.content: Risk management creates and protects value by contributing to the achievement of objectives and improvement of performance. The following principles form the foundation:
bullets: Value creation and protection through informed decision-making
Integration into organisational processes at all levels
Structured and comprehensive approach to consistent outcomes
Customisation to the organisation's external and internal context
Inclusive engagement of stakeholders for diverse knowledge and views
Dynamic responsiveness to emerging and changing risks
Best available information as the basis for decisions
Human and cultural factors influencing all aspects of risk management
Continual improvement through learning and experience

sections.heading: 5. Risk Management Framework (ISO 31000 Cl. 5)
sections.content: The risk management framework provides the foundations and organisational arrangements for designing, implementing, monitoring, reviewing, and continually improving risk management throughout the organisation.

sections.heading: 5.1 Leadership and Commitment
sections.content: Top management and oversight bodies shall demonstrate leadership and commitment by ensuring risk management is integrated into all organisational activities, allocating appropriate resources, and establishing risk appetite and tolerance levels.

sections.heading: 5.2 Integration
sections.content: Risk management shall be a part of, and not separate from, the organisational purpose, governance, leadership and commitment, strategy, objectives, and operations.

sections.heading: 5.3 Design
sections.content: The framework shall be designed considering the organisation's external and internal context, risk management policy, accountability and oversight, resource allocation, and communication and consultation mechanisms.

sections.heading: 5.4 Implementation
sections.content: Implementation shall include developing an appropriate plan including timelines and resources, identifying where, when, and how decisions are made, and modifying applicable decision-making processes.

sections.heading: 5.5 Evaluation and Improvement
sections.content: The organisation shall periodically measure framework performance against its purpose, plans, indicators, and expected behaviour, and determine whether it remains suitable to support achieving objectives.

sections.heading: 6. Risk Appetite and Tolerance
sections.content: The organisation defines risk appetite statements for each risk category. These thresholds determine acceptable residual risk levels and escalation triggers:

sections.heading: 7. Roles and Responsibilities
sections.content: Clear accountability for risk management is essential:

rows: Approve risk policy, set risk appetite, oversee framework effectiveness
Design and maintain risk framework, report to board, coordinate risk activities
Review risk register, monitor KRIs, approve treatment plans, escalate issues
Identify, assess, and manage risks within their area, implement controls
Report risks and incidents, follow risk procedures, participate in training

sections.heading: 8. Review and Approval
sections.content:
This policy shall be reviewed at least annually or following significant organisational changes, incidents, or audit findings. The review shall assess policy effectiveness, alignment with ISO 31000:2018, and changes in the external and internal context.

All amendments require approval from the Managing Director.

sections.heading: 1. Purpose
sections.content: This document defines the organisation's risk appetite — the amount and type of risk it is prepared to pursue, retain, or take in order to achieve its objectives. It provides the basis for risk-based decision-making and ensures consistent application of risk tolerance thresholds across all functions.

sections.heading: 2. Definitions
sections.content: The following terms are used in accordance with ISO Guide 73:2009:
bullets: Risk Appetite: The amount and type of risk an organisation is willing to pursue or retain
Risk Tolerance: The organisation's readiness to bear the risk after risk treatments, in order to achieve its objectives
Risk Capacity: The maximum amount of risk the organisation can absorb
Risk Threshold: A level of risk above which risks are not tolerable regardless of benefits

sections.heading: 3. Overall Risk Appetite
sections.content:
The organisation adopts a balanced approach to risk, recognising that some risk-taking is necessary to achieve strategic objectives. The overall risk appetite is MODERATE — the organisation is willing to accept a moderate level of risk in pursuit of its goals, provided risks are identified, assessed, and managed within defined tolerance levels.

Risks to health, safety, and regulatory compliance are subject to a VERY LOW appetite — these risks must be minimised to as low as reasonably practicable (ALARP).

sections.heading: 4. Risk Appetite by Category
sections.content: Each risk category has a defined appetite level with corresponding scoring thresholds based on the 5×5 risk matrix (score range 1-25):

sections.heading: 4.1 Health & Safety — VERY LOW
sections.content:
Maximum Tolerable Score: 6 | Acceptable Residual: 3 | Escalation Threshold: 8

The organisation has zero tolerance for preventable harm. All health and safety risks must be reduced to ALARP. Any risk scoring above 6 requires immediate escalation and a documented treatment plan.

sections.heading: 4.2 Environmental — VERY LOW
sections.content:
Maximum Tolerable Score: 6 | Acceptable Residual: 3 | Escalation Threshold: 8

Environmental risks including pollution, emissions, and waste must be minimised. Regulatory non-compliance is unacceptable.

sections.heading: 4.3 Compliance & Legal — VERY LOW
sections.content:
Maximum Tolerable Score: 4 | Acceptable Residual: 3 | Escalation Threshold: 6

Regulatory and legal compliance risks must be maintained at the lowest achievable level. Zero tolerance for deliberate non-compliance.

sections.heading: 4.4 Financial — MODERATE
sections.content:
Maximum Tolerable Score: 12 | Acceptable Residual: 8 | Escalation Threshold: 15

Moderate financial risk is acceptable where aligned with strategy and within budget parameters. Material financial exposures require board awareness.

sections.heading: 4.5 Operational — LOW
sections.content:
Maximum Tolerable Score: 9 | Acceptable Residual: 6 | Escalation Threshold: 12

Operational disruptions should be minimised through robust controls and business continuity planning.

sections.heading: 4.6 Technology & Cyber — LOW
sections.content:
Maximum Tolerable Score: 8 | Acceptable Residual: 6 | Escalation Threshold: 10

Cyber risks demand proactive management. Critical system downtime and data breaches are not tolerable.

sections.heading: 4.7 Strategic — MODERATE
sections.content:
Maximum Tolerable Score: 12 | Acceptable Residual: 9 | Escalation Threshold: 15

Strategic risks are inherent in growth and transformation. Controlled risk-taking aligned with strategy is encouraged.

sections.heading: 4.8 Reputational — LOW
sections.content:
Maximum Tolerable Score: 9 | Acceptable Residual: 6 | Escalation Threshold: 12

Reputation damage can have lasting effects. Risks to brand, stakeholder confidence, and public trust must be carefully managed.

sections.heading: 5. Appetite Status Classification
sections.content: Each risk is classified against its category's appetite thresholds:

rows: Monitor per review frequency; no additional action
Residual score > acceptable but ≤ maximum tolerable
Immediate escalation; mandatory treatment plan; report to Risk Committee

sections.heading: 6. Monitoring and Review
sections.content:
Risk appetite statements shall be reviewed:

a) At least annually by the Risk Committee
b) Following any significant incident or near-miss
c) When strategic objectives change
d) After material changes in the external environment

The Chief Risk Officer shall report appetite breaches to the Board quarterly.

sections.heading: 7. Approval
sections.content: This Risk Appetite Statement is approved by the Board of Directors and is effective from the date of approval.

sections.heading: 1. Purpose
sections.content: This procedure describes the systematic process for identifying, analysing, and evaluating risks in accordance with ISO 31000:2018 Clause 6.4 (Risk Assessment). It ensures consistent application of risk assessment methodology across the organisation.

sections.heading: 2. Scope
sections.content: This procedure applies to all risk assessments conducted within the organisation including:
bullets: Risks imported from other IMS modules (COSHH, FRA, incidents, audits)

sections.heading: 3. Risk Assessment Process (ISO 31000 Cl. 6.4)
sections.content: Risk assessment is the overall process of risk identification, risk analysis, and risk evaluation.

sections.heading: 3.1 Risk Identification (Cl. 6.4.2)
sections.content:
The purpose of risk identification is to find, recognise, and describe risks that might help or prevent the organisation achieving its objectives.

Methods include:
bullets: Automatic import from linked IMS modules (COSHH assessments, fire risk assessments, incident investigations, audit findings)

sections.heading: 3.2 Risk Analysis (Cl. 6.4.3)
sections.content:
Risk analysis involves understanding the nature, sources, and causes of identified risks and estimating the level of risk.

The 5×5 Risk Matrix is used:

rows: Rare — May occur only in exceptional circumstances
Unlikely — Could occur at some time
Possible — Might occur at some time
Likely — Will probably occur in most circumstances
Major — Serious impact requiring significant response
Almost Certain — Expected to occur in most circumstances

sections.heading: 3.3 Risk Scoring
sections.content:
Risk Score = Likelihood × Consequence (range 1-25)

Both inherent risk (before controls) and residual risk (after controls) are assessed:

sections.heading: 3.4 Risk Evaluation (Cl. 6.4.4)
sections.content:
Risk evaluation involves comparing the results of risk analysis with the established risk appetite (RM-POL-002) to determine whether the risk and its magnitude are acceptable or tolerable.

Risks scoring above the category's maximum tolerable score are classified as EXCEEDS APPETITE and require mandatory treatment planning and escalation.

sections.heading: 4. Control Assessment
sections.content: Existing controls are documented and assessed for effectiveness:

rows: Control is well-designed, consistently applied, and demonstrably effective
Control is reasonably effective but has minor gaps
Control has significant gaps or is inconsistently applied
Control is absent, ineffective, or not applied

sections.heading: 5. Documentation
sections.content: All risk assessments shall be recorded in the Enterprise Risk Register using form RM-FRM-001 or the Nexara IMS digital platform. The record shall include:
bullets: Risk title, description, causes, risk event, and consequences

sections.heading: 6. Review Frequency
sections.content: Risk assessments shall be reviewed at the following intervals:

sections.heading: 1. Purpose
sections.content: This procedure defines the process for selecting and implementing risk treatment options in accordance with ISO 31000:2018 Clause 6.5 (Risk Treatment). It ensures risks exceeding appetite thresholds are systematically addressed through appropriate treatment strategies.

sections.heading: 2. Treatment Options (ISO 31000 Cl. 6.5.2)
sections.content: The following treatment options are available. Selection should be based on the risk level, cost-benefit analysis, and alignment with organisational objectives:

rows: Eliminate the risk by not starting or continuing the activity
Unacceptable risk where no viable treatment exists
Implement controls to decrease the probability of occurrence
Where preventive measures can significantly lower frequency
Implement controls to minimise the impact if the risk materialises
Where impact mitigation is more practical than prevention
Share the risk with other parties (insurance, outsourcing, JVs)
Where risk can be effectively distributed to parties better able to manage it
Retain the risk by informed decision, within appetite
Where residual risk is within appetite after analysis
Increase exposure to capture upside opportunity
Positive risks/opportunities where benefit outweighs downside

sections.heading: 3. Treatment Plan Requirements
sections.content: Each risk requiring treatment shall have a documented treatment plan including:
bullets: Specific treatment actions with responsible owners
Escalation criteria if treatment is ineffective

sections.heading: 4. Treatment Action Tracking
sections.content: Treatment actions are tracked in the Nexara IMS platform with the following fields:

rows: Clear description of the treatment action
Detailed explanation of what is required

sections.heading: 5. Approval and Escalation
sections.content: Treatment plans for risks with the following levels require specific approval:

sections.heading: 6. Monitoring and Review
sections.content:
Treatment effectiveness shall be monitored through:

a) Regular review of treatment action status and due dates
b) Reassessment of residual risk scores after treatment implementation
c) Key Risk Indicator (KRI) monitoring for treated risks
d) Post-treatment audit verification

The Risk Committee shall review all active treatment plans quarterly.

sections.heading: 1. Purpose
sections.content: This procedure defines the process for establishing, monitoring, and responding to Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) in accordance with ISO 31000:2018 Clause 6.6 (Monitoring and Review). KRIs provide early warning signals that risk levels are changing and may require management action.

sections.heading: 2. KRI Definition
sections.content: A Key Risk Indicator is a quantitative metric that provides an early signal of increasing risk exposure. Effective KRIs are:
bullets: Measurable — quantifiable with a clear unit of measurement
Relevant — directly linked to a specific risk
Timely — capable of providing early warning before risk materialises
Comparable — consistent measurement over time to identify trends
Actionable — threshold breaches trigger specific management responses

sections.heading: 3. KRI Status Thresholds
sections.content: Each KRI has three threshold levels:

rows: KRI value is within acceptable range
KRI value has breached the warning threshold
Investigate trend; consider preventive action; notify risk owner
KRI value has breached the critical threshold
Immediate investigation; implement corrective action; escalate to Risk Committee

sections.heading: 4. KRI Measurement Process
sections.content: The following process shall be followed for each KRI measurement cycle:

sections.heading: 4.1 Data Collection
sections.content: KRI data shall be collected at the defined frequency (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, or annually) from reliable and verified sources. Automated data collection through the Nexara IMS platform is preferred where available.

sections.heading: 4.2 Recording
sections.content: Each reading shall be recorded with the following information:
bullets: Notes or context for the reading

sections.heading: 4.3 Trend Analysis
sections.content: KRI readings shall be analysed for trends over time. Where a KRI shows a deteriorating trend (even within GREEN thresholds), the risk owner shall be notified and the risk reassessed.

sections.heading: 5. Escalation Protocol
sections.content: KRI breaches trigger the following escalation:

rows: Risk Committee informed; treatment plan reviewed
Immediate escalation to CRO; emergency risk review

sections.heading: 6. KRI Review
sections.content:
The KRI portfolio shall be reviewed quarterly to ensure:

a) KRIs remain relevant to current risks
b) Thresholds are appropriately calibrated
c) Measurement frequencies are adequate
d) New risks have appropriate KRIs assigned

The Risk Committee shall approve all changes to KRI definitions and thresholds.

sections.heading: 1. Purpose
sections.content: This procedure describes the bow-tie analysis methodology used for HIGH and above rated risks, in accordance with ISO 31010:2019 (Risk Assessment Techniques). Bow-tie analysis provides a visual representation of the relationship between causes, the risk event, consequences, and the controls in place.

sections.heading: 2. When to Perform Bow-Tie Analysis
sections.content: Bow-tie analysis shall be performed for:
bullets: All risks rated HIGH (score 8-12) or above
Risks identified as critical by the Risk Committee
As directed by regulatory requirements or audit findings

sections.heading: 3. Bow-Tie Structure
sections.content: A bow-tie diagram consists of the following elements:

sections.heading: 3.1 Hazard/Threat (Left Side)
sections.content: The source of potential harm or the threatening condition. Multiple threats may be identified for a single risk event.

sections.heading: 3.2 Top Event (Centre)
sections.content: The unwanted event — the point at which control is lost. This is the risk event described in the risk register.

sections.heading: 3.3 Consequences (Right Side)
sections.content: The potential outcomes if the top event occurs. Multiple consequences should be identified covering different impact types (safety, financial, reputational, etc.).

sections.heading: 3.4 Preventive Controls (Left Barriers)
sections.content: Controls that prevent the threat from causing the top event. These reduce the likelihood of the risk materialising.

sections.heading: 3.5 Mitigating Controls (Right Barriers)
sections.content: Controls that limit the consequences once the top event has occurred. These reduce the severity of impact.

sections.heading: 4. Bow-Tie Development Process
sections.content: Follow these steps:
numberedList: Define the top event (risk event from the risk register)
Identify all credible threats/causes that could lead to the top event
Identify all potential consequences of the top event
Map existing preventive controls against each threat
Map existing mitigating controls against each consequence
Identify gaps where additional controls are needed
Document escalation factors that could defeat controls
Record in the Nexara IMS bow-tie library

sections.heading: 5. Documentation
sections.content: Bow-tie analyses shall be recorded with:
bullets: Link to parent risk register entry
JSON-structured diagram data (threats, consequences, preventive controls, mitigating controls)
Each element with description and type classification
Date of analysis and analyst name
Review date (aligned with risk review frequency)

sections.heading: 6. Review and Update
sections.content:
Bow-tie analyses shall be reviewed:

a) At each risk review cycle
b) Following any incident related to the risk
c) When controls are modified or added
d) When new threats or consequences are identified

Updated bow-ties shall be presented to the Risk Committee for critical risks.

sections.heading: 1. Purpose
sections.content: This procedure defines the process for reviewing risks, recording review outcomes, and reporting risk information to stakeholders in accordance with ISO 31000:2018 Clauses 6.6 (Monitoring and Review) and 6.7 (Recording and Reporting).

sections.heading: 2. Review Types
sections.content: The following review types are defined:

rows: Regular reassessment of risk status, controls, and scoring
Response to incident, near-miss, or material change
Comprehensive review of risk category or portfolio
Assessment of overall risk management effectiveness

sections.heading: 3. Review Process
sections.content: Each risk review shall:
numberedList: Confirm the risk is still relevant and correctly described
Reassess inherent and residual likelihood and consequence
Evaluate control effectiveness (have controls improved or deteriorated?)
Review treatment plan progress and action completion
Check risk appetite status (WITHIN / AT_LIMIT / EXCEEDS)
Determine if the risk level has changed
Document the review outcome and set next review date

sections.heading: 4. Review Record
sections.content: Each review shall be recorded with:
bullets: Residual score at time of review

sections.heading: 5. Reporting
sections.content: Risk reports shall be produced at the following levels:

sections.heading: 5.1 Operational Reports
sections.content: Monthly reports to department managers covering:

sections.heading: 5.2 Risk Committee Reports
sections.content: Quarterly reports to the Risk Committee covering:

sections.heading: 5.3 Board Reports
sections.content: Quarterly/annual reports to the Board covering:

sections.heading: 6. Overdue Review Escalation
sections.content: Reviews overdue by more than 14 days shall be escalated:

rows: Report to CRO; risk flagged as unmanaged

sections.content: Complete all sections below for each risk identified. Fields marked with * are mandatory.

rows: [Detailed description of the risk and its context]

sections.heading: Section B: Inherent Risk Assessment (Before Controls)

rows: ☐ 1 Rare  ☐ 2 Unlikely  ☐ 3 Possible  ☐ 4 Likely  ☐ 5 Almost Certain
☐ LOW (1-3)  ☐ MEDIUM (4-6)  ☐ HIGH (8-12)  ☐ VERY HIGH (15-19)  ☐ CRITICAL (20-25)

sections.heading: Section D: Residual Risk Assessment (After Controls)

rows: ☐ 1 Rare  ☐ 2 Unlikely  ☐ 3 Possible  ☐ 4 Likely  ☐ 5 Almost Certain
☐ LOW (1-3)  ☐ MEDIUM (4-6)  ☐ HIGH (8-12)  ☐ VERY HIGH (15-19)  ☐ CRITICAL (20-25)
☐ Avoid  ☐ Reduce Likelihood  ☐ Reduce Consequence  ☐ Share/Transfer  ☐ Accept  ☐ Exploit

sections.heading: 3. Context Setting (ISO 31000 Cl. 6.3)
sections.content: Record the external and internal context discussed:

sections.heading: 4. Risks Identified
sections.content: Record each risk identified during the workshop:

rows:
☐ STRONG — All controls well-designed and operating effectively
☐ ADEQUATE — Most controls effective with minor gaps
☐ WEAK — Significant control gaps exist
☐ NONE — Controls absent or fundamentally ineffective

sections.heading: 2. Bow-Tie Elements
sections.content: Complete each section of the bow-tie diagram:

rows: [The unwanted event — point at which control is lost]

sections.heading: 1. Executive Summary
sections.content:
This report provides a comprehensive overview of the organisation's risk profile for the period [DD/MM/YYYY] to [DD/MM/YYYY]. It summarises key risk metrics, material changes, and recommendations for the Board's attention.

Reporting Period: [Quarter/Year]
Report Date: [DD/MM/YYYY]
Prepared By: [Chief Risk Officer]

sections.content: For each risk requiring treatment, complete the following:

sections.heading: 5. Monitoring Arrangements
sections.content:
Treatment plan progress shall be monitored through:

a) Weekly action tracking by risk owners
b) Monthly progress review by Risk Manager
c) Quarterly reporting to Risk Committee
d) Post-treatment effectiveness review

The Risk Manager shall report any blocked or overdue actions to the CRO within 5 working days.
